name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا ۚ وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِۦ دَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُوسُفَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٤)
```

```english
And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them. And Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon, Job, Joseph, Moses and Aaron—thus do We reward the virtuous— (84)
```

```arabic
وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Ilyas—each of them among the righteous— (85)
```

```arabic
وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَٱلْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٦)
```

```english
and Ishmael, Elisha, Jonah and Lot—each We graced over all the nations— (86)
```

```arabic
وَمِنْ ءَابَآئِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّـٰتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَٰنِهِمْ ۖ وَٱجْتَبَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٨٧)
```

```english
and from among their fathers, their descendants and brethren—We chose them and guided them to a straight path. (87)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ يَهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۚ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٨٨)
```

```english
That is Allah’s guidance: with it, He guides whomever He wishes of His servants. But were they to ascribe any partners [to Allah], what they used to do would not avail them. (88)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ۚ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا۟ بِهَا بِكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٩)
```

```english
They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood. So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will never disbelieve in them. (89)
```

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَبِهُدَىٰهُمُ ٱقْتَدِهْ ۗ قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا ۖ إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٩٠)
```

```english
They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance. Say, ‘I do not ask you any recompense for it. It is just an admonition for all the nations.’ (90)
```

## (Commentary)

The connection of these verses to what preceded them is clear and requires no explanation; they are a continuation of the discourse regarding Ibrahim (peace be upon him). Although the verses contain an expression of favor upon him and those prophets mentioned alongside him—as is evident in His saying, the Exalted: **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ** "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob" (6:84), and His saying: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ** "and thus do We reward the virtuous" (6:84), and His saying: **وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ** "and each We exalted above all the worlds" (6:86), among others—they are not intended for that purpose alone, as some commentators suggest. Rather, they are intended to clarify the immense blessings and beautiful divine favors that are followed by primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري) — *the absolute oneness of Allah as recognized by the innate human disposition* — and guidance through divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية).

This is consistent with the objective of this Surah, which elucidates the matter of monotheism (التوحيد) according to what is guided by the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — upon which mankind was created. It has been previously mentioned that the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), in relation to the preceding verses of the Surah, serves as a practical example set forth for a general explanation.

In the context of these verses, in addition to the exposition of monotheism, there is a clarification that the creed of monotheism is preserved among people in a continuous chain whose links are joined one to another through divine guidance and special Lordly care. Through this, Allah preserved the divine primordial nature from being lost to satanic whims or falling entirely from the state of potentiality into nothingness, which would render the purpose of creation void and cause it to go to waste. This is indicated by His saying: **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ** "And We gave him..." and His saying: **وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ** "and Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring..." and His saying: **وَمِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَإِإِخْوَانِهِمْ** "and from among their fathers, their offspring, and their brethren" (6:87), and His saying: **فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ** "So if these disbelieve in them..." (6:89).

Within these verses is also an explanation of the characteristics that distinguish divine guidance from others, namely: election (الاجتباء), the uprightness of the path (استقامة الصراط), and the bestowing of the Book (الكتاب), judgment (الحكم), and prophethood (النبوة), as will be explained later, if Allah wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا** — "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and each We guided" (6:84). Ishaq is the son of Ibrahim, and Ya'qub is the son of Ishaq (peace be upon them). In His saying: **كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا** "each We guided," the word "each" (كلا) is placed first to indicate that divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) pertained to every single one of those mentioned independently. It does not mean that it pertained to some independently, like Ibrahim, and to others merely as followers. It is as if it were said: "We guided Ibrahim, and We guided Ishaq, and We guided Ya'qub," as has been noted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ** — "and Noah We had guided before" (6:84). This suggests that the chain of guidance is neither broken nor did it begin with Ibrahim (peace be upon him); rather, the mercy before him encompassed Nuh (peace be upon him).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ** — "and from his offspring, David and Solomon..." until His saying: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "and thus do We reward the virtuous" (6:84). The pronoun in "his offspring" (ذريته) apparently refers to Nuh, because he is the closest antecedent in the text, and because among those listed are those who are not from the offspring of Ibrahim, such as Lut and Ilyas, according to what has been said.

It has also been suggested that the pronoun refers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him), and that Lut and Ilyas (peace be upon them) were mentioned as "offspring" by way of predominance (تغليبا), as He said: **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِ النُّبُوَّةَ وَالْكِتَابَ** — "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and We ordained prophethood and the Book among his descendants" (29:27). Or, it is suggested that "offspring" refers only to the six mentioned in this verse and not the rest. As for His saying: **وَزَكَرِيَّا** "and Zechariah..." and His saying: **وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ** "and Ishmael...", they would be conjoined to His saying: **وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ** "and from his offspring" and not to His saying: **دَاوُودَ** "David...". However, this is far-fetched given the context.

As for His saying: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ** "and thus do We reward the virtuous," it appears that this "reward" refers to the aforementioned divine guidance. This is indicated by the word "thus" (كذلك). The use of the distal demonstrative pronoun is to magnify the status of this guidance. It is similar to His saying: **كَذَٰلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللَّهُ الْأَمْثَالَ** — "Thus does Allah draw parables" (13:17). The meaning is: We reward the virtuous according to this pattern.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Elias—each was among the righteous" (6:85). The discussion regarding the meaning of virtue (الإحسان) and righteousness (الصلاح) has preceded in earlier sections. The mention of 'Isa among those listed from the offspring of Nuh (peace be upon him)—while he is only connected to him through his mother Maryam—is a clear indication that the Noble Quran considers the children of daughters and their descendants to be true children and offspring. We have previously derived similar conclusions from the verse of inheritance and the verse of prohibited marriages. Further discussion on this will follow in the upcoming tradition-based discourse (البحث الروائي), if Allah the Exalted wills.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ** — "and Ishmael, Elisha, Jonah and Lot—each We exalted above all the worlds" (6:86). It appears that "Ishmael" refers to the son of Ibrahim and the brother of Ishaq (peace be upon them). His saying: **الْيَسَعَ** (al-Yasa') is pronounced with two *fathahs* like *asad*, and it is also recited as *al-Layasa'* like *daygham*. He is one of the prophets of the Children of Israel. Allah mentioned his name alongside Isma'il (peace be upon him) as in His saying: **وَاذْكُرْ إِسْمَاعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ ۖ وَكُلٌّ مِّنَ الْأَخْيَارِ** — "And remember Ishmael, Elisha and Dhul-Kifl—each was among the elected" (38:48). He did not mention anything of his story in His speech.

As for His saying: **وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ** "and each We exalted above all the worlds," the term **world** (العالم) refers to a group of people, such as the world of the Arabs, the world of the Persians, or the world of the Romans. The meaning of exalting them above all the worlds is to prioritize them in rank over the people of their time, because special divine guidance (الهداية الخاصة الإلهية) reached them without intermediary. As for others, the mercy of guidance only reaches them through their mediation. It is also possible that the meaning is exalting them—insofar as they are a group guided by primordial divine guidance without intermediary—over all the worlds of mankind, whether they were their contemporaries or not. For divine guidance without intermediary is a blessing by which the one characterized by it precedes those who are not. This encompassed the mentioned prophets and those who joined them from their fathers, offspring, and brethren; thus, the community formed by them is exalted over all others by a divine exaltation.

In short, the criterion for this exaltation is being characterized by that divine guidance in which there is no intermediary. The prophets were exalted over others because of being characterized by it. If we were to assume that others besides them were characterized by this guidance—such as the angels, as might appear from His speech, the Exalted, or the Imams, as preceded in the discussion of His saying: **وَإِذِ ابْتَلَىٰ إِبْرَاهِيمَ رَبُّهُ بِكَلِمَاتٍ** "And when his Lord tested Abraham with certain words" (2:124) in the first volume of this book—then the prophets (peace be upon them) would not be exalted over them from this specific aspect, even if they might be exalted over them from another aspect besides guidance.

From here it becomes clear that the argument used by some to prove that prophets are superior to angels based on this verse is misplaced. It also becomes clear that the intended exaltation is only from the perspective of the special divine guidance that reached them without the mediation of anyone. As for their being the people of election (الاجتباء), the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم), and the people of the Book, judgment, and prophethood, that is a matter outside the focus of the exaltation mentioned in this verse.

Know that the order in which Allah mentioned these prophets by name in these three verses—seventeen prophets in total—did not follow their chronological order, which is obvious, nor their order of rank and merit. For among them are Nuh, Musa, and 'Isa (peace be upon them), who are superior to the rest of those mentioned by the explicit text of the Book, as preceded in the discussions on prophethood in the second volume of this book, yet others were mentioned before them.

The author of *al-Manar* mentioned regarding the order of the prophets named in these three verses—fourteen prophets—what can be summarized as follows: Allah divided them into three categories based on common themes that unite each group.

The first category: Dawud, Sulayman, Ayyub, Yusuf, Musa, and Harun. The common theme among them is that Allah gave them kingship, authority, judgment, and sovereignty along with prophethood and messengership. He began by mentioning Dawud and Sulayman, who were wealthy and favored kings. After them, He mentioned Ayyub and Yusuf; Ayyub was a wealthy and great virtuous prince, and Yusuf was a great minister and an influential ruler. Both were tested with adversity and were patient, and with prosperity and were grateful. After that, Musa and Harun, who were rulers over their people but were not kings.

Each pair of these three pairs is distinguished by a specific merit. Furthermore, the order is based on worldly blessings: Dawud and Sulayman enjoyed more of them than Ayyub and Yusuf, and they more than Musa and Harun. Or, the order is based on religious merit: it is apparent that Musa and Harun are superior to Ayyub and Yusuf, and they are superior to Dawud and Sulayman because they combined patience in adversity and gratitude in prosperity.

The second category: Zakariyya, Yahya, 'Isa, and Ilyas. These were distinguished by intense asceticism (الزهد) in the world, turning away from its pleasures, and a lack of desire for its adornments. Therefore, He specifically described them here as "the righteous" (الصالحين) because this description is most fitting for them when compared to others, even though every prophet is absolutely righteous and virtuous.

The third category: Isma'il, al-Yasa', Yunus, and Lut. Their mention was delayed because they lacked a specific distinguishing feature; they did not possess the worldly kingship and authority of the first group, nor the extreme turning away from the world of the second group. (End of summary).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Razi, there is something similar, though what the former mentioned is more plausible than what al-Razi mentioned. A critique of both their views is that they made the third category consist of those who have no distinguishing feature, which is incorrect. Isma'il (peace be upon him) was tested by Allah with the matter of the sacrifice, and he was patient with what Allah tested him with. He, the Exalted, said: **فَبَشَّرْنَاهُ بِغُلَامٍ حَلِيمٍ فَلَمَّا بَلَغَ مَعَهُ السَّعْيَ قَالَ يَا بُنَيَّ إِنِّي أَرَىٰ فِي الْمَنَامِ أَنِّي أَذْبَحُكَ فَانظُرْ مَاذَا تَرَىٰ ۚ قَالَ يَا أَبَتِ افْعَلْ مَا تُؤْمَرُ ۖ سَتَجِدُنِي إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ** — "So We gave him the good news of a forbearing son. When he was old enough to assist him, he said, ‘My son! I see in a dream that I am sacrificing you. See what you think.’ He said, ‘Father! Do whatever you have been commanded. If Allah wishes, you will find me to be patient.’" until He said: **إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَهُوَ الْبَلَاءُ الْمُبِينُ وَفَدَيْنَاهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ وَتَرَكْنَا عَلَيْهِ فِي الْآخِرِينَ** — "This was indeed a manifest test. Then We ransomed him with a great sacrifice, and left for him a good name among posterity" (37:101-108). This is one of the magnificent distinctions with which Allah specifically honored Isma'il (peace be upon him)—a manifest test by which he was distinguished, to the extent that Allah made the sacrifice in the Hajj a general act of obedience commemorating his trial for the sake of Allah, and left for him a good name among posterity. Moreover, he shared with his noble father in building the Ka'bah, and that is distinction enough.

Similarly, the Prophet Yunus (peace be upon him) was tested by Allah with something He did not test any other prophet with: being swallowed by the whale, whereupon he called out in the darknesses: "There is no god but You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!"

As for Lut, his trials for the sake of Allah are mentioned in the Noble Quran. He endured trials at the beginning of his affair with Ibrahim (peace be upon him) until he migrated from his people and land in his company. Then Allah sent him to the people of Sodom and its surroundings, the cradle of an indecency (الفحشاء) that no one in the worlds had preceded them in, until when destruction encompassed them, not a single house of Muslims was found among them except the house of Lut, excluding his wife.

As for al-Yasa', no story is mentioned for him in the Quran. However, it is mentioned in some narrations that he was the legatee (وصي) of Ilyas and that he brought to his people what 'Isa son of Maryam (peace be upon him) brought, such as raising the dead and healing the blind and the leper, and that Allah tested his people with a great year of drought and famine.

Thus, it is better to complete the aforementioned explanation for the order of the names listed in the verse by saying: The first group mentioned—six in number—were distinguished by kingship and leadership along with messengership. The second group—four in number—were distinguished by asceticism in the world and turning away from its trappings. The third group—four in number—possessed various characteristics and great divine trials, each distinguished by something specific. And Allah knows best.

Furthermore, what he mentioned in the course of his speech regarding the superiority of Musa and Harun over Ayyub and Yusuf, and their superiority over Dawud and Sulayman based on the reason he cited, as well as his defining righteousness (الصلاح) as asceticism and virtue (الإحسان) as such—all of that is rejected and lacks evidence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَانِهِمْ** — "and from among their fathers, their offspring, and their brethren" (6:87). This expression supports what we previously stated: that the objective is to clarify the continuity of the chain of guidance, as He conjoined the rest to those mentioned by stating they are connected to them through fatherhood, sonship, or brotherhood.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاجْتَبَيْنَاهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَاهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِراطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** — "and We chose them and guided them to a straight path" (6:87). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "It is said: **جبيت** (I collected) the water in the basin, meaning I gathered it. The basin that gathers it is called a **جابية** (gathering-pool), and its plural is **جواب** (pools). Allah the Exalted said: **وَجِفَانٍ كَالْجَوَابِ** 'and basins like pools' (34:13). From this, the term was borrowed for **جبيت** (I collected) the land tax (الخراج). From this also is His saying, the Exalted: **يُجْبَىٰ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَرَاتُ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ** 'to which are brought the fruits of all things' (28:57). **الاجتباء** (Election/Choosing) is gathering by way of selection (الاصطفاء). Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **فَاجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ** 'Then his Lord chose him' (68:50)."

He continued: "Allah's election (اجتباء) of a servant is His singling him out with a divine outpouring through which various types of blessings are attained for him without the servant's own effort. This is for the prophets and some of those close to them among the truthful (الصديقين) and the witnesses (الشهداء), as He the Exalted said: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ يَجْتَبِيكَ رَبُّكَ** 'That is how your Lord will choose you' (12:6), and **فَاجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَجَعَلَهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** 'Then his Lord chose him and made him one of the righteous' (68:50), and **وَاجْتَبَيْنَاهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَاهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ** 'and We chose them and guided them to a straight path' (6:87). And His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ** 'Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him' (20:122). And Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **يَجْتَبِي إِلَيْهِ مَن يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَن يُنِيبُ** 'Allah chooses for it whomever He wishes, and He guides to it whomever returns penintent' (42:13)." (End of quote).

The meaning of election (الاجتباء) that he mentioned—even if it is so according to what its occurrences in His speech, the Exalted, suggest—is a necessary consequence of the original meaning in terms of its application to His action regarding them. What the context of the verses provides is that the care pertained to the original meaning of the word, which is "gathering" from different, scattered places and locations. This serves as a preparation for what is mentioned afterward regarding guidance to a straight path. It is as if He is saying: "We gathered them despite their being scattered, until when they were gathered and joined one to another, We guided them all to such-and-such a path."

This is because, as you have known, the purpose of the context is to clarify the continuity of the chain of those guided by this primordial divine guidance. It is appropriate for this that they be conceived as a gathered and unified group so that the divine mercy encompasses their collective, and they are guided together by a single guidance that leads them to a single straight path (صراط مستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — in which there is no disagreement at all. Thus, it does not differ according to circumstances, nor according to times, nor according to parts, nor according to the individuals traveling upon it, nor according to the destination.

This is because their path to which Allah guided them—even if it differs in the outward form of the religious laws (الشرائع) in terms of breadth and narrowness—this difference is only according to brevity and detail, and the lesser or greater capacity of the nations. All are united in one reality, which is primordial monotheism and the servitude (العبودية) to which the human constitution guides, according to the type of creation that Allah, the Glorified, manifested upon that. It is well known that the human creation, insofar as it is human creation, does not change or transform in a way that necessitates a change in the principles of human consciousness and will. Man's outward senses, his inward feelings and emotions, and the principle of judgment and decree within him—which is the primordial intellect (العقل الفطري)—continue to function according to their principles in a single manner, even if opinions and objectives differ according to the gradual perfection pertaining to the species and the awareness of the various needs of life.

Thus, man continues to feel his need for food, drink, clothing, shelter, and marriage; he desires what brings rest to his acquisitive soul and dislikes what pains or harms him; he hopes for the happiness of life and fears wretchedness and an evil end, even if the manifestations of his life and the forms of his actions differ from age to age and generation to generation.

He, the Exalted, said: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا ۚ فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind (there is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know)" (30:30). Thus, the upright divine religion (الدين الحنيف) that is the maintainer of human society is that to which the primordial nature guides and toward which the human creation inclines, according to what it perceives of its existential needs and what it is inspired with regarding what brings it happiness in terms of belief and action—or in other words, in terms of knowledge, ethics, and deeds.

This is a matter that does not change or transform because it is built upon the ontological primordial nature (الفطرة التكوينية) for which there is no way to change or transform. Thus, it does not differ according to circumstances and times—such that it would call to human happiness in one state but not another, or in one time but not another—nor according to its parts, such that some rulings of the upright religion would conflict with others through contradiction, opposition, or anything else that leads to the nullification of one by the other. For all of them are nourished from the breast of monotheism (التوحيد), which balances them in the best possible way, just as when the bodily faculties conflict or some of them seek to overpower others, there is a governing governor who manages each according to its weight and influence in sustaining human life.

Nor is it according to individuals, for those guided by this precious, primordial guidance (الهداية الفطرية) — *the innate leading toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — do not differ in their path. The last of them calls to nothing other than what the first of them called to, even if their call varied in brevity or detail according to the different eras of humanity in its perfection and progress, as the Exalted says: **إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ** — "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam" (3:19), and He says: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it.’" (42:13).

Nor is it according to the objective and goal, for it is monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — to which the scattered religious sciences, virtuous ethics, and legal rulings return. The Exalted says: **إِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ** — "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me." (21:92), and He says: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ** — "We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me.’" (21:25).

From what has preceded, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **وَهَدَيْناهُمْ إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — "and We guided them to a straight path" (6:87) has become clear. He mentioned "path" (صراط) in the indefinite form without referring to it as a previously known entity, as in His saying: **اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed" (1:6-7), so that the mind's attention would be directed toward its attribute of rectitude (الاستقامة). Rectitude in a thing is its remaining upon a single, consistent course in its quality and property. Thus, the path to which they were guided is a path in which there is no discrepancy in any respect or under any circumstance, because it is a path built upon the primordial nature (الفطرة). Just as the human primordial nature — which is the mode of his creation and being — does not vary insofar as it is a human creation in its guidance and its being guided toward the ontological objectives (المقاصد التكوينية) of man.

Therefore, these people who are guided to the straight path (مستقيم الصراط) are in divine security (أمن إلهي) from the perils of the journey and the pitfalls of the road. This is because the path they traverse and the course they strike out upon contains no discrepancy between guidance and misguidance, truth and falsehood, or felicity and wretchedness. Rather, its parts are harmonious and its states are equal; it stands upon the truth and leads to the truth. It does not leave its companion in confusion, nor does it bring him to injustice, misery, or disobedience. The Exalted says: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the guided ones." (6:82).

His saying, the Exalted: **ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ** — "That is Allah’s guidance: with it He guides whomever He wishes of His servants" (6:88) until the end of the verse. The Exalted explains that the description of guidance He mentioned, by which He guided those mentioned among His prophets, is the identifier of His specific guidance by which He guides whomever He wishes of His servants.

For guidance is only guidance — in the true sense of guidance — if it is from Allah, the Glorified. And guidance is only the guidance of Allah if it brings the one characterized by it to a straight path (صراطاً مستقيماً) upon which the possessors of guidance, namely the honored prophets (عليه‌السلام), have agreed to enter. Furthermore, the parts of that path must agree in calling to the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد), establishing the call to truth, and being marked with the mark of servitude (العبودية) and godwariness (التقوى).

As for the path in which a distinction is made between the messengers of Allah, such that some are believed in and others are disbelieved, or a distinction is made between the rulings and laws of Allah, such that some are taken and others are abandoned; and those paths that do not guarantee the felicity of human social life or lead to that which does not contain human felicity — those are the paths in which there is no pleasure for Allah, the Glorified. They have deviated from the law of the primordial nature (شريعة الفطرة) into the depths of misguidance and the slippery slopes of whims (الأهواء). Being guided to them is not being guided by the guidance of Allah, the Glorified.

The Exalted says: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِاللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يُفَرِّقُوا بَيْنَ اللهِ وَرُسُلِهِ وَيَقُولُونَ نُؤْمِنُ بِبَعْضٍ وَنَكْفُرُ بِبَعْضٍ وَيُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا بَيْنَ ذلِكَ سَبِيلاً ، أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ حَقًّا** — "Those who deny Allah and His apostles and seek to separate Allah from His apostles, and say, ‘We believe in some and deny others,’ and seek to take a way in between—it is they who are truly faithless" (4:150-151). And He says: **أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ الْكِتابِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ فَما جَزاءُ مَنْ يَفْعَلُ ذلِكَ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْيٌ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلى أَشَدِّ الْعَذابِ** — "Do you believe in part of the Book and deny another part? So what is the requital of those of you who do that except disgrace in the life of this world? And on the Day of Resurrection they shall be consigned to the severest punishment" (2:85). And He says: **وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنِ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ بِغَيْرِ هُدىً مِنَ اللهِ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "And who is more astray than him who follows his desires without any guidance from Allah? Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot." (28:50). He means that the path containing the following of whims is nothing but misguidance that does not bring its traveler to the felicity of life, and it is not the guidance of Allah because it contains injustice (ظلم), and Allah, the Glorified, has not made injustice — and will never make it — a means to attain felicity, nor is felicity attained through injustice.

In short, the guidance of Allah, the Glorified, is characterized by the fact that it does not contain misguidance, nor does it coexist with misguidance by leading to it. Rather, it is pure guidance followed by pure felicity, a gift that is never cut off (عطاء غير مجذوذ). However, this is not like the gifts customary among us, where the ownership of the giver (المعطي) is severed from his gift and transferred to the receiver (المعطى), who then possesses it regardless of whether he is grateful or ungrateful.

Rather, this divine gift is predicated upon the condition of monotheism (التوحيد) and servitude (العبودية). Thus, no one has any special honor before Him, the Exalted, nor any security from Him, except through pure servitude. For this reason, He concluded the discourse by saying: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and had they ascribed partners [to Him], what they used to do would have come to nothing for them." (6:88). He mentioned polytheism (الإشراك) because the pivot of the explanation is monotheism.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ** — "They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood" (6:89). The reference using a word denoting distance (أُولئِكَ) is to indicate the loftiness of their status and the height of their station. What is meant by giving them the Book and the rest is giving it to them as a collective group, even if some of them were not given some of the mentioned items, as passed in the commentary (تفسير) of His saying: **وَاجْتَبَيْناهُمْ وَهَدَيْناهُمْ** — "and We chose them and guided them" (6:87). For the Book was only given to some of the prophets, such as Noah, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus (عليه‌السلام).

When the Book (الكتاب) is attributed in the speech of the Exalted to the prophets (عليه‌السلام) in a general sense, it refers to the scriptures (الصحف) that contain the laws (الشرائع) and by which judgment is made among people regarding that in which they differ. This is like His saying, the Exalted: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed" (2:213). And His saying: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَنُورٌ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ** — "We sent down the Torah containing guidance and light. The prophets... would judge by it" (5:44) until He said: **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَمُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** — "We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it. So judge between them by what Allah has sent down" (5:48), among other verses.

**Judgement (الحكم)** is the establishing of a predicative relationship (النسبة التصديقية) between the parts of a sentence, such as our saying: "So-and-so is a scholar." When this occurs in social matters and practical issues that circulate among members of society, this type of relationship is considered a "judgment" (حكماً), just as the proposition itself is called a judgment. For example, it is said: "It is obligatory (يجب) for a person to do such-and-forth," "it is prohibited (يحرم) for him," "it is permissible (يجوز)," or "I like" or "dislike that you do such-and-forth." Thus, obligation, prohibition, permissibility, recommendation, and dislike are called judgments (أحكاماً), just as the propositions containing them are called judgments. Members of society have other judgments arising from other relationships, such as ownership (الملك), leadership (الرئاسة), representation (النيابة), sufficiency (الكفاية), authority (الولاية), and others.

When the infinitive meaning is intended, it refers to the act of originating the judgment and establishing it. This is either according to legislation and law-making (التشريع والتقنين), as legislators establish sound judgments for people to follow and act upon in the course of their lives to preserve the order of their society; or it is according to discernment and opinion (التشخيص والنظر), such as the discernment of judges and rulers in disputes and lawsuits that the property belongs to so-and-so and the right is with so-and-so, and like the discernment of the givers of legal opinions (أهل الفتيا) in their fatwas. Sometimes it refers to the enforcement of the judgment (إنفاذ الحكم), such as the judgment of a governor or king over the people regarding what they desire within the domain of authority and sovereignty.

What is apparent regarding "judgement" (الحكم) in the verse, by the context of the Book being mentioned with it, is that it refers to the meaning of adjudication (القضاء). Thus, the meaning of giving the Book and judgement is the granting of the laws of religion and adjudicating according to them among the people. This is the apparent meaning of several verses, such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ** — "and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed" (2:213); and His saying: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَنُورٌ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا** — "We sent down the Torah containing guidance and light. The prophets, who had submitted, would judge by it" (5:44); and His saying: **لِتَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِما أَراكَ اللهُ** — "so that you may judge between people by what Allah has shown you" (4:105); and His saying: **وَداوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ إِذْ يَحْكُمانِ فِي الْحَرْثِ** — "And [remember] David and Solomon when they gave judgement concerning the tillage" (21:78); and His saying: **يا داوُدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ وَلا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوى فَيُضِلَّكَ عن سَبِيلِ اللهِ** — "O David! Indeed We have made you a vicegerent on the earth. So judge between people with justice, and do not follow your desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah." (38:26), among many other verses. This is the case even if a verse like His saying, recounting the words of Abraham (عليه‌السلام): **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ** — "My Lord! Grant me judgement, and join me with the righteous" (26:83) does not, in its literal sense, preclude being interpreted in the broader meaning.

As for prophethood (النبوة), it has already been mentioned in the commentary of His saying: **كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ** — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets" (2:213) that it refers to the realization of the news of the Unseen (أنباء الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — through a specific divine care. These are the reports related to what is beyond the senses and the sensible, such as His oneness, the Exalted, the angels, and the Last Day.

The enumeration of these three honors with which Allah, the Glorified, graced the lineage of the prophets (عليه‌السلام) — namely the Book, judgement, and prophethood — in the context of the verses describing His guidance, indicates that they are among the effects of Allah's guidance. Through them, knowledge of Allah, the Exalted, and His signs is completed. It is as if it were said: That guidance upon which We gathered the prophets (عليه‌السلام) and by which We preferred them over all the worlds (العالمين) is that which brings them to a straight path, teaches them the Book containing His laws, directs them and appoints them to judge among the people, and informs them of the news of the Unseen.

## (A Discussion on the Meaning of "the Book" in the Quran)

The Book (الكتاب), according to what first comes to our minds today, is the leaf or leaves in which a group of meanings is recorded by way of marking with a pen, a press, or otherwise. However, since the consideration in the use of names is the purposes for which the naming occurred, this allows for an expansion in applying names to things other than their familiar named objects at the time of coinage. The purpose of a "book" is to record a group of meanings such that a person can recall them whenever he refers back to it. This meaning is not inseparable from what the hand has marked with a pen upon paper. Just as the "book" in a person's memory when he has memorized it is a book, and when he dictates it from memory it is a book, even if there are no leaves or tablets marked with the familiar pen.

Based on this expansion, His speech, the Exalted, has proceeded in applying "the Book" to a portion of the revelation (الوحي) delivered to the Prophet, especially if it contains a firm decree (عزيمة) and a law (شريعة). Likewise, it is applied to that which records events and occurrences with a type of recording with Allah, the Glorified. The Exalted says: **كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبارَكٌ** — "[This is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you" (38:29). And the Exalted says: **ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَها** — "No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about" (57:22). And the Exalted says: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً ، اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ** — "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find wide open. ‘Read your book!’" (17:13-14).

What Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned in His speech of a "book" attributed to Himself is confined to these three categories, other than what is apparent in His saying regarding the matter of the Torah: **وَكَتَبْنا لَهُ فِي الْأَلْواحِ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ مَوْعِظَةً وَتَفْصِيلاً لِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "And We wrote for him in the tablets advice concerning all things and an elaboration of all things" (7:145); and His saying: **وَأَلْقَى الْأَلْواحَ وَأَخَذَ بِرَأْسِ أَخِيهِ** — "and he threw down the tablets and seized his brother by the head" (7:150); and His saying: **وَلَمَّا سَكَتَ عَنْ مُوسَى الْغَضَبُ أَخَذَ الْأَلْواحَ وَفي نُسْخَتِها هُدىً وَرَحْمَةٌ لِلَّذِينَ هُمْ لِرَبِّهِمْ يَرْهَبُونَ** — "When Moses’ anger abated, he took up the tablets whose inscription contained guidance and mercy for those who are in awe of their Lord." (7:154).

The first category: The Books revealed to the prophets (عليه‌السلام), which are those containing the laws of religion — as previously mentioned. Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned among them the Book of Noah (عليه‌السلام) in His saying: **وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ** — "and He sent down with them the Book with the truth" (2:213); and the Book of Abraham and Moses (عليه‌السلام), saying: **صُحُفِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى** — "the scriptures of Abraham and Moses" (87:19); and the Book of Jesus, which is the Gospel (الإنجيل), saying: **وَآتَيْناهُ الْإِنْجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدىً وَنُورٌ** — "and We gave him the Gospel, within which is guidance and light" (5:46); and the Book of Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), saying: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ وَقُرْآنٍ مُبِينٍ** — "These are the signs of the Book and a manifest Quran" (15:1); and saying: **رَسُولٌ مِنَ اللهِ يَتْلُوا صُحُفاً مُطَهَّرَةً ، فِيها كُتُبٌ قَيِّمَةٌ** — "an apostle from Allah reciting purified scriptures, containing upright writings" (98:2-3); and saying: **فِي صُحُفٍ مُكَرَّمَةٍ ، مَرْفُوعَةٍ مُطَهَّرَةٍ ، بِأَيْدِي سَفَرَةٍ ، كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ** — "[It is] in honored scriptures, exalted and purified, by the hands of envoys, noble and pious" (80:13-16); and saying: **نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ ، عَلى قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ ، بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ** — "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down to your heart, so that you may be one of the warners, in a clear Arabic language." (26:193-195).

The second category: The Books that record the deeds of the servants, whether good or evil. Among them are those specific to each human soul, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً** — "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book" (17:13); and His saying: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done" (3:30), among other verses. Among them are those that record the deeds of a nation (الأمة), as indicated by His saying: **وَتَرى كُلَّ أُمَّةٍ جاثِيَةً كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى إِلى كِتابِهَا** — "And you will see every nation kneeling. Every nation will be summoned to its book" (45:28). And among them are those in which all people share, as in His saying: **هذا كِتابُنا يَنْطِقُ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْحَقِّ إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "This is Our record; it speaks against you with the truth. Indeed We used to record whatever you used to do" (45:29), if the address therein is to all people.

Perhaps this category of the Book has another division according to the division of people into the two groups of the pious (الأبرار) and the vicious (الفجار), which is what He mentions in His saying: **كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتابَ الفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ ، وَما أَدْراكَ ما سِجِّينٌ ، كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ** — "No indeed! The record of the vicious is in Sijjin. And what will show you what Sijjin is? It is a written record" (83:7-9) until He says: **كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتابَ الْأَبْرارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ ، وَما أَدْراكَ ما عِلِّيُّونَ ، كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ ، يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — "No indeed! The record of the pious is in Illiyyun. And what will show you what Illiyyun is? It is a written record, witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]." (83:18-21).

The third category: The Books that record the details of the order of existence and the events occurring within it. Among them is the Book protected from change (المصون عن التغير) in which everything is written, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ مِنْ مِثْقالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي السَّماءِ وَلا أَصْغَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** — "Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book" (10:61); and His saying: **وَكُلَّ شَيْءٍ أَحْصَيْناهُ فِي إِمامٍ مُبِينٍ** — "and We have figured everything in a manifest Imam" (36:12); and His saying: **وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ** — "and with Us is a preserving Book" (50:4); and His saying: **لِكُلِّ أَجَلٍ كِتابٌ** — "Every time has a [prescribed] writing" (13:38). Among the terms (الآجال) is the stated term (الأجل المسمى) for which there is no way to change. And His saying: **وَما كانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَنْ تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ كِتاباً مُؤَجَّلاً** — "No soul may die except by Allah’s leave, at an appointed time." (3:145).

Perhaps this type of Book is divided into a single, general Book that preserves all events and existents, and a specific Book for every single existent by which its state in existence is preserved, as suggested by the last two verses and other noble verses that resemble them.

Among them are the Books to which change may occur and which are subject to erasure and affirmation (المحو والإثبات), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** — "Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes and with Him is the Mother of the Book" (13:39). The exhaustive investigation of each of these categories of Books is deferred to the appropriate place in this work, and Allah is the One whose help is sought.

## (A Discussion on the Meaning of "Judgement" in the Quran)

The linguistic root of the term "judgment" (الحكم), based on what is gathered from its various usages, is "prevention" (المنع). For this reason, a master’s command is called a "judgment" (حكماً) because the command prevents the subordinate from having absolute freedom in will and action, bridling him from pursuing whatever his soul desires. Similarly, "judgment" in the sense of adjudication (القضاء) prevents the subject of dispute from being destabilized by contention and quarreling, or from being corrupted by transgression and injustice. Likewise, "judgment" in the sense of affirmation (التصديق) prevents a proposition from being susceptible to doubt.

The terms "perfection" (الأحكام) and "fortification" (الاستحكام) suggest a state in a thing that prevents the entry of what might corrupt its parts or the intrusion of a foreign element into its interior. In one respect, "perfection" (الأحكام) is the opposite of "detailing" (التفصيل), which is to make a thing part by part, thereby undoing the cohesion and unity of its components. The Exalted says: **كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ** "A Book whose signs have been perfected and then elaborated, from One [who is] All-wise, All-aware" (Hud: 1). To this root returns the meaning of the "decisive" (المحكم) — *the clear and unambiguous verse that forms the foundation of the Book* — which stands in contrast to the "metaphorical" (المتشابه).

Al-Raghib says in *al-Mufradat*: "**حكم** (H-K-M): Its root is 'prevention' for the sake of rectification. From this, the bridle of a beast is called the *hakamah* (حكمة) [with two *fathahs*]. It is said: 'I judged him' (حكمته), meaning I restrained the beast with the bridle. 'I perfected it' (أحكمتها) means I provided it with a bridle. Similarly, 'I judged the ship' and 'I perfected it' (أحكمتها)." He then cited the poet: "O sons of Hanifah, restrain (أحكموا) your fools." End quote.

When "judgment" (الحكم) is attributed to Allah, the Glorified, if it pertains to the creative realm (تكوين), it conveys the meaning of existential decree (القضاء الوجودي) — *the divine act of bringing into being that is synonymous with true existence and external reality across its various levels*. The Exalted says: **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ** "Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment" (al-Ra‘d: 41).

He also says: **وَإِذا قَضى أَمْراً فَإِنَّما يَقُولُ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ** "When He decides on a matter, He just says to it, ‘Be!’ and it is" (al-Baqarah: 117). From this perspective, His saying is understood: **قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا كُلٌّ فِيها إِنَّ اللهَ قَدْ حَكَمَ بَيْنَ الْعِبادِ** "The arrogant will say, ‘We are all in it [together]. Indeed Allah has judged between [His] servants’" (al-Mu’min: 48).

If it pertains to the legislative realm (تشريع), it conveys the meaning of law-giving (التقنين) and masterly command (الحكم المولوي). The Exalted says: **وَعِنْدَهُمُ التَّوْراةُ فِيها حُكْمُ اللهِ** "while they have the Torah, containing Allah’s judgment" (al-Ma’idah: 43), and He says: **وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللهِ حُكْماً** "and who is better than Allah in judgment" (al-Ma’idah: 50).

When attributed to the Prophets (عليه السلام), it conveys the meaning of adjudication (القضاء), which is one of the divine offices with which He honored them. The Exalted says: **فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ عَمَّا جاءَكَ مِنَ الْحَقِّ** "So judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires against the truth that has come to you" (al-Ma’idah: 48). He also says: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْناهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ** "They are the ones whom We gave the Book, the judgement" (al-An‘am: 89).

Perhaps in some verses there is an indication or evidence of their being granted "judgment" (الحكم) in the sense of legislation (التتشريع), as in His saying recounting the supplication of Ibrahim (عليه السلام): **رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَأَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ** "My Lord! Grant me [the capacity to] judge, and unite me with the righteous" (al-Shu‘ara’: 83).

As for people other than the Prophets, "judgment" is attributed to them in the sense of adjudication (القضاء), as in His saying: **وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ الْإِنْجِيلِ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ فِيهِ** "Let the people of the Gospel judge by what Allah has sent down in it" (al-Ma’idah: 47). It is also attributed to them in the sense of [unauthorized] legislation (التشريع), for which Allah censured them, as in His saying: **وَجَعَلُوا لِلَّهِ مِمَّا ذَرَأَ مِنَ الْحَرْثِ وَالْأَنْعامِ نَصِيباً فَقالُوا هذا لِلَّهِ بِزَعْمِهِمْ وَهذا لِشُرَكائِنا** — until He says — **ساءَ ما يَحْكُمُونَ** "They assign to Allah a share of the crops and the livestock He has created, and say, ‘This is for Allah,’ so they claim, ‘and this is for our partners.’ ... Evil is the judgment they make" (al-An‘am: 136). And His saying: **وَإِنَّ وَعْدَكَ الْحَقُّ وَأَنْتَ أَحْكَمُ الْحاكِمِينَ** "and Your promise is true, and You are the fairest of all judges" (Hud: 45). This verse, according to its context, includes "judgment" in the sense of fulfilling the promise and executing the decree.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنْ يَكْفُرْ بِها هؤُلاءِ فقد وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ** "So if these [people] disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them" (al-An‘am: 89). The two pronouns in His saying "disbelieve in them" (يَكْفُرْ بِها) and "entrusted them" (وَكَّلْنا بِها) refer to "guidance" (الهدى). It may be treated as either masculine or feminine because it is a form of guiding (هداية). Alternatively, they refer to the Book (الكتاب), the judgment (الحكم), and the prophethood (النبوة), which are among the effects of divine guidance. The first of these two views is not without some far-fetchedness. The ones referred to by "these" (هؤُلاءِ) are the disbelievers among the people of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who rejected the call. Those certain to be included, based on the context of the verse, are the disbelievers of Mecca, whom Allah, the Glorified, referred to by saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَأَنْذَرْتَهُمْ أَمْ لَمْ تُنْذِرْهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ** "As for the faithless, it is the same to them whether you warn them or do not warn them, they will not have faith" (al-Baqarah: 6).

The meaning according to the first view is: If the polytheists of your people disbelieve in Our guidance — which is Our path — then We have entrusted it to those among Our servants who do not disbelieve in it. Disbelief and belief relate to guidance, especially when it means the "path" (الطريقة), just as they are attributed to Allah and His signs. The Exalted says: **وَأَنَّا لَمَّا سَمِعْنَا الْهُدى آمَنَّا بِهِ** "When we heard the guidance, we believed in it" (al-Jinn: 13), and He says: **فَمَنْ تَبِعَ هُدايَ فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ولا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ** "then whoever follows My guidance, there shall be no fear upon them, nor shall they grieve" (al-Baqarah: 38).

According to the second view: If the polytheists of Mecca disbelieve in the Book, the judgment, and the prophethood — which encompass the divine path and the religious call — then We have entrusted them to a people who are not disbelievers in them.

As for who these "people" (قَوْماً) are — and the use of the indefinite noun indicates their great significance — the opinions of the commentators have differed:

Some say they are the Prophets mentioned in the preceding verses, who are eighteen in number, or the Prophets in general, whether mentioned by name or by description in His saying: **وَمِنْ آبائِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَإِخْوانِهِمْ** "and from among their fathers, their descendants and their brethren" (al-An‘am: 87). However, the context of the phrasing does not support this, as the apparent meaning of His saying "who will not disbelieve in them" (لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ) is a negation of the present state or a continuous negation. The mentioned Prophets (عليه السلام) were not present at the time of the address. Had that been the intended meaning, it would have been necessary to say: "they *were* not disbelievers in them." Furthermore, the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is not counted among them in this specific regard, even though he is one of them and the most excellent of them, for Allah, the Glorified, mentions him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) afterward by saying: **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللهُ فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** "They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance" (al-An‘am: 90).

Others say they are the angels. This is countered, as has been noted, by the fact that the word "people" (القوم), especially when used without qualification, is not applied to angels and does not readily come to mind. Moreover, the context of the verse provides a kind of consolation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and there is little sense in consoling him over the disbelief of his people by citing the faith of the angels.

Some say they are the believers who were with him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) at the time the Surah was revealed in Mecca, or the Emigrants (المهاجرون) in general. This is countered by the fact that some of these individuals apostatized after their belief, such as the one who said, "I will send down the like of what Allah has sent down," whose case the Glorified addressed later in this Surah. Furthermore, there were hypocrites among them, so the description "who will not disbelieve in them" (لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ) does not apply to them collectively.

Others say they are the Helpers (الأنصار), or the Emigrants and the Helpers together, or the Companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from both groups, who established this call and supported the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the hour of difficulty, and whom Allah praised most eloquently in His Book. This is countered by the fact that while the nobility of their group and the loftiness of their station as a collective is beyond doubt, there were among them those who apostatized after their belief and hypocrites whose true state had not yet been revealed. Such a description does not fit those of this character, especially since the verse says: **فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ** "We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will not disbelieve in them." The literal sense is that disbelief has no way to reach them; He did not say: "We have entrusted them to a people who believe in them" or "who have believed in them."

It may be gathered from the words of some that the meaning is the establishment of faith within their collective, even if it is possible for individuals among them to fail in upholding it. In other words, the phrase "who will not disbelieve in them" (لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ) is a description of the community (المجتمع), and the departure of some individuals does not contradict the community being characterized by a quality that resides in the whole as a whole. The believers in him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from the Helpers, or from them and the Emigrants, or the Companions, were those in whom faith was firmly established without vanishing, even if it vanished from some of their individual members.

If this view were sound, it would indicate that the "people" refers to the entire Muslim nation (الأمة المسلمة) or the believers from all nations, as there is no evidence to restrict it to one people over another. The fact that some possess specific religious merits and honors — such as the precedence of the Emigrants in faith and their patience under persecution for the sake of Allah, or the Helpers' taking of the home and faith and their raising the word of monotheism (التوحيد) — only necessitates the excellence of their characterization by this trait, not its exclusivity to them or the deprivation of others who share in its meaning.

However, this view is challenged by the fact that the familiar style of His speech regarding social attributes that do not encompass every individual of a society is to exclude those who fail to meet them if such individuals exist, or to use phrasing that carries the meaning of an exception. For example: **لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ ، ثُمَّ رَدَدْناهُ أَسْفَلَ سافِلِينَ ، إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** "We certainly created man in the best of forms; then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, except those who have faith" (al-Tin: 4-6).

And His saying, the Exalted: **مُحَمَّدٌ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَماءُ بَيْنَهُمْ** — until He said — **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ مِنْهُمْ مَغْفِرَةً وَأَجْراً عَظِيماً** (Muhammad is the Apostle of Allah, and those who are with him are hard against the faithless and merciful amongst themselves... Allah has promised those of them who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. [48:29]); and His saying: **إِنَّ الْمُنافِقِينَ فِي الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ مِنَ النَّارِ** — until He said — **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَاعْتَصَمُوا بِاللهِ وَأَخْلَصُوا دِينَهُمْ لِلَّهِ** (Indeed the hypocrites will be in the lowest reach of the Fire... except those who repent and reform, and hold fast to Allah and dedicate their religion purely to Allah. [4:145-146]); and His saying: **كَيْفَ يَهْدِي اللهُ قَوْماً كَفَرُوا بَعْدَ إِيمانِهِمْ** — until He said — **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا** (How shall Allah guide a people who have lapsed into unfaith after their faith... except those who repent after that and reform. [3:86-89]). This meaning is frequent and recurrent in the Noble Quran; so why is it that in His saying: **قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ** (a people who are not disbelievers in them), He did not exclude those among the people who fail to meet this description, despite their existence among them?

Even stranger than this is the claim of some that the description of the people as being those who "are not disbelievers in them" — the people (القوم) being the Helpers (الأنصار) according to this view — is an indication that although they had not yet believed in them, they had not disbelieved in them in the manner that the polytheists of Mecca had. In addition to the fact that this view does not escape the aforementioned objections raised against previous interpretations, the people of Medina from the Helpers (الأنصار) were, at the time of the revelation of these verses, polytheists (مشركين) worshipping idols. There is no sense in negating disbelief (الكفر) from them, unless it means rejection after being called (الرد بعد الدعوة), which is arrogance (الاستكبار) and disdain (الاستنكاف). However, there is no evidence that disbelief (الكفر) in this verse carries this specific meaning, especially since the verses are framed to describe divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) as the opposite of polytheism (الإشراك), as seen in the flow of His saying: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** (But if they had ascribed any partners [to Him], what they used to do would have surely come to naught. [6:88]). Furthermore, the entrustment (التوكيل) mentioned in the verse implies the meaning of preservation (الحفظ), and there is no sense in saying: "If these disbelieve in them, We have preserved them with a people who have neither believed in them nor yet rejected them."

Others have said: The intended people are the non-Arabs (العجم), who did not believe in them at that time. This seems to be derived from the saying of the Exalted: **إِنْ يَشَأْ يُذْهِبْكُمْ أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ وَيَأْتِ بِآخَرِينَ** (If He wishes, He will take you away, O mankind, and bring others [in your place]. [4:133]), as it has been narrated that the "others" refers to the non-Arabs (العجم). However, this is subject to the same objections as the previous view.

Still others say: The intended people are the believers from the nation (أمة) of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or the believers from all nations. To this, it must be said that the same objections raised against the preceding views apply here as well. Yes, it is possible to justify this by saying that the intended ones are individuals from this nation or from all nations who believe in Allah with a faith (إيمان) that is never followed by disbelief (كفر) as long as they live in this world. These would be a believing people who are not disbelievers in them, and although disbelief is not impossible for them, their persistence in faith (الإيمان) in the call to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — without disbelief or hypocrisy (نفاق) warrants the truth of His saying "a people who are not disbelievers in them" (قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ) regarding them. This would complete the meaning of the verse in that it is intended to console the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and comfort his noble heart, as he was grieved by the disbelief of the polytheists among his people and their arrogance in responding to the call of truth and faith in Allah and His signs. It also indicates His pride, the Exalted, in the preservation of His guidance and His way (طريقته) with which He honored His honored servants and His near-stationed prophets.

However, the objection to this is that this view is built upon a coincidental matter (قضية اتفاقية), which is the faith of the believers remaining sound and free from decline by mere chance, without a guarantor (ضامن) to ensure its persistence. This does not harmonize with the saying of the Exalted: **وَكَّلْنا بِها** (We have entrusted them), for entrustment (التوكيل) implies reliance (الاعتماد) and includes the meaning of preservation (الحفظ) and protection (الكلاءة). There is no reason for pride or boasting in a matter for which there is no guarantor for its stability nor a preserver for its constancy and survival.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, censures much of what is called faith (الإيمان) when He says: **وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ** (And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him. [12:106]). These verses only describe pure primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري) and the pure, pristine divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) free from the taint of polytheism (الشرك) and injustice (الظلم), with which Allah honored His friend (خليله) Abraham and the honored prophets (عليه السلام) before and after him. This is as Abraham (عليه السلام) mentions in his saying, as recounted by Allah, the Glorified: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** (Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided. [6:82]). Guidance of such a nature cannot consider every person who merely dons the mantle of faith as its preserver or as one entrusted (موكلاً) with it by Allah to protect it from loss and corruption, especially when among them are tyrants, transgressors, pharaohs, the arrogant, the oppressive, the people of innovation (أهل البدع), and those deeply immersed in immorality and various types of lewdness and depravity.

What ought to be said regarding the meaning of the verse — namely His saying: **فَإِنْ يَكْفُرْ بِها هؤُلاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ** (So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who are not disbelievers in them. [6:89]) — is that since the verses describe primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري) and divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) pure from the taint of ascribing partners to Allah, and mention that Allah honored a continuous and united chain of His prophets with this guidance, choosing them as "offspring one from another," selecting them, and guiding them to a straight path (صراط مستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah* — in which there is no straying, and granting them the Book, the Judgment, and Prophethood.

He then branched from that His saying: **فَإِنْ يَكْفُرْ بِها هؤُلاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنا بِها قَوْماً لَيْسُوا بِها بِكافِرِينَ**. The context is one of pride from Him, the Exalted, and consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to comfort his soul, so that the grief he witnesses from his people's disbelief, arrogance, and wandering blindly in their transgression does not weaken him or dissolve his resolve in the religious call. The meaning is: Do not be grieved by what you see of their disbelief in this divine guidance and the way (الطريقة) comprised of the Book, the Judgment, and Prophethood, which We granted to the chain of guided ones among the noble prophets. For We have entrusted them to a people who are not disbelievers in them; thus, there is no way for this divine guidance to be lost or perish, because We have entrusted them with it, relied upon them for it, and they are absolutely not disbelievers in it.

These are a people for whom disbelief (كفر) is inconceivable and into whose hearts polytheism (شرك) does not enter, because Allah has entrusted them with it, relied upon them for it, and preserved it through them. If polytheism were possible for them or if failure were possible from them, then relying upon them for it would be an error and misguidance, and Allah, the Glorified, neither errs nor forgets.

The verse indicates — and Allah knows best — that for Allah, the Glorified, in every age, there is a servant or servants entrusted with the divine guidance and the straight path (الطريقة المستقيمة) contained in what He gave His prophets of the Book, the Judgment, and Prophethood. Through them, Allah preserves His religion (دين) from vanishing and His guidance from extinction. Polytheism and injustice find no path to them due to their being protected by a divine protection (عصمة إلهية); they are the people of inerrancy (أهل العصمة) among the noble prophets and their successors (عليه السلام).

Thus, the verse is specific to the people of inerrancy (أهل العصمة). The furthest one could expand this is to include the righteous among the believers who have held fast to the protection of godwariness (تقوى) and righteousness, and pure faith from polytheism and injustice, thereby exiting the authority of Satan. The Exalted said: **إِنَّهُ لَيْسَ لَهُ سُلْطانٌ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَلى رَبِّهِمْ يَتَوَكَّلُونَ** (Indeed he has no authority over those who have faith and put their trust in their Lord. [16:99]), provided it is true of them that Allah has entrusted them with it and relied upon them for it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللهُ فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** (They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance. [6:90]). He returned a second time to defining them by that which defines divine guidance (الهدى الإلهي). For divine guidance does not fail in its function and effect, which is bringing one to the goal. The Exalted said: **فإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُّ** (Indeed Allah does not guide those who mislead. [16:37]).

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded in His saying: **فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** to **emulate** (الاقتداء) — which is following — their guidance, not them personally, because his law (شريعة) abrogates their laws and his Book is a guardian (مهيمن) over their books. Furthermore, this guidance mentioned in the verses has no intermediary in it between Him, the Exalted, and the one He guides. As for the attribution of guidance to them in His saying: **فَبِهُداهُمُ** (their guidance), it is merely an honorific attribution. The evidence for this is His saying: **ذلِكَ هُدَى اللهِ** (That is Allah’s guidance... [6:88]).

Some have used this verse as evidence that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his nation were bound by the laws of those before them, except where evidence of abrogation exists. To this, it is said: That would only be valid if it had been said: "So emulate them" (فبهم اقتده). As for His saying: **فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** (So follow their guidance), it is far from indicating that, as is apparent.

The Glorified concluded His speech describing primordial monotheism (التوحيد الفطري) and the divine guidance toward it by saying, addressing His Prophet: **قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى لِلْعالَمِينَ** (Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it. It is only a reminder for all the nations.’ [6:90]). It is as if it were said: Be guided by the divine guidance by which the prophets before you were guided, and remind all the worlds (العالمين) with it without asking them for a reward for that. Say that to them to put their souls at ease, for it is more successful for the call and further from suspicion. Allah, the Glorified, has recounted this statement from Noah and the prophets after him (عليه السلام) in their calls.

The term "reminder" (الذكرى) is more emphatic than "mention" (الذكر), as mentioned by al-Raghib. In the verse is evidence for the universality of his prophethood (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to all the worlds.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Qisas al-Anbiya* by al-Tha'labi: It is narrated that Elijah (إلياس) came to the house of a woman from the Children of Israel who had a son named Elisha (اليسع) son of Khatub. The boy was suffering from an affliction, so she sheltered him and hid his matter. Elijah prayed for him, and he was cured of the affliction he had. Elisha then followed Elijah, believed in him, accepted his truthfulness, and stayed with him, going wherever he went. Then he mentioned the story of Elijah's ascension, and that Elisha called out to him at that moment: "O Elijah, what do you command me?" So he threw his cloak down to him from the high atmosphere, and that was the sign of his appointment as his successor over the Children of Israel.

He said: And Allah, the Exalted, granted prophethood to Elisha (عليه السلام) by His grace, and sent him as a prophet and messenger to the Children of Israel. Allah, the Exalted, revealed to him and supported him with the like of what He supported His servant Elijah. So the Children of Israel believed in him; they used to revere him and defer to his opinion and command, and the judgment of Allah remained established among them until Elisha departed from them.

In *al-Bihar*, from *al-Ihtijaj*, *al-Tawhid*, and *al-Uyun*, in a long report narrated by al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Nawfali from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام): In what he argued against the Catholicos of the Christians, until he (عليه السلام) said: **إن اليسع قد صنع مثل ما صنع عيسى عليه‌السلام ـ مشى على الماء وأحيى الموتى وأبرأ الأكمه والأبرص ـ فلم يتخذه أمته ربا** — "Indeed, Elisha performed the like of what Jesus (عليه السلام) performed: he walked on water, revived the dead, and healed the blind and the leper, yet his nation did not take him as a lord." (The report).

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl from al-Thumali from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام): Regarding His saying: **وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ كُلًّا هَدَيْنا** (And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them [6:84]) — "so that We might place it [the guidance/imamate] in his household (أهل بيته)"; **وَنُوحاً هَدَيْنا مِنْ قَبْلُ** (and Noah We had guided before [6:84]) — "so that We might place it in his household. Thus He commanded the succession (العقب) from the progeny of the prophets—those who were before Abraham and for Abraham."

**I say:** In this is support for what we previously stated: that the verses are for the purpose of explaining the continuity of the chain of guidance.

In *al-Kafi*, with a chain of transmission, and in *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, without a chain, from Bashir al-Dahhan from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), who said: **والله لقد نسب الله عيسى بن مريم في القرآن ـ إلى إبراهيم من قبل النساء ثم تلا : (وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِ داوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ) إلى آخر الآية وذكر عيسى** — "By Allah, Allah has indeed attributed Jesus son of Mary in the Quran to Abraham through the women." Then he recited: **وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِ داوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ** until the end of the verse, and it mentions Jesus.

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, from Abu Harb from Abu al-Aswad, who said: Al-Hajjaj sent for Yahya ibn Ma'mar and said: "It has reached me that you claim that al-Hasan and al-Husayn are from the progeny (ذرية) of the Prophet, and that you find this in the Book of Allah, yet I have read the Book of Allah from beginning to end and have not found it." He replied: "Do you not read Surah al-An'am: **وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِ داوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ** until it reaches **وَيَحْيى وَعِيسى**?" He said: "Is not Jesus from the progeny of Abraham?"

He said: "Yes, I have read it."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from ibn Abi Hatim from Abu al-Harb ibn Abi al-Aswad similarly.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Hakim, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Abd al-Malik ibn Umayr, who said: Yahya ibn Ma'mar entered upon al-Hajjaj, and al-Husayn was mentioned. Al-Hajjaj said: "He was not from the progeny of the Prophet (ص)." Yahya said: "You have lied." He said: "Then bring me clear evidence for what you have said." So he recited: **وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِهِ داوُدَ وَسُلَيْمانَ** until His saying **وَعِيسى وَإِلْياسَ**. Thus, the Exalted informed that Jesus is from the progeny of Abraham through his mother. He said: "You have spoken the truth."

**I say:** Al-Alusi mentioned in *Ruh al-Ma'ani* regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَعِيسى** (and Jesus): "In the mention of him (عليه السلام) is evidence that 'progeny' (الذرية) includes the children of daughters, because his lineage is only from the side of his mother." An objection was raised against this: that he has no father to attribute his lineage to instead of the mother, so the analogy of others to him in being the progeny of the maternal grandfather is not apparent. This was countered by saying that the requirement of his being without a father is that he be mentioned in the context of progeny. In this is a manifest prohibition, and the matter is one of disagreement. Those who hold that the daughter's son is included in the progeny use this verse as evidence, and with it Musa al-Kadhim (رضي الله عنه) argued, according to what some have narrated, before al-Rashid.

In *al-Tafsir al-Kabir*: Abu Ja'far (رضي الله تعالى عنه) argued with it before al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, and also with the Verse of Mubahalah (آية المباهلة) where he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called al-Hasan and al-Husayn (رضي الله تعالى عنهما) after the revelation of: **تَعالَوْا نَدْعُ أَبْناءَنا وَأَبْناءَكُمْ** (Come! Let us call our sons and your sons... [3:61]). Some claimed that this is among his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) specific characteristics. The legal opinions (إفتاء) of our companions have differed on this issue, and that which I incline toward is the view of inclusion. (End of quote).

In *al-Manar*, he said: I say: Regarding this chapter is the hadith of Abu Bakrah in *al-Bukhari* attributed to the Prophet: **أن ابني هذا سيد** — "Indeed, this son of mine is a leader," meaning al-Hasan. The word "son" (ابن) is not used by the Arabs for the children of daughters. Also, the hadith of Umar in the book *Ma'rifat al-Sahabah* by Abu Nu'aym, attributed to the Prophet: **وكل ولد آدم فإن عصبتهم لأبيهم ـ خلا ولد فاطمة فإني أبوهم وعصبتهم** — "Every child of Adam has their agnate relationship (عصبة) to their father, except the children of Fatimah, for I am their father and their agnate relative." People have followed this, saying of the children of Fatimah (عليه السلام): "The children of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), his sons, his progeny (عترته), and his household (أهل بيته)." (End of quote).

**I say:** There is confusion in this matter, and it has become unclear to several prominent scholars who thought the issue was a linguistic one (لفظية) to be followed according to the language, to the point that some argued using the words of the poet:

*Our sons are the sons of our sons, but our daughters—*
*Their sons are the sons of distant men.*

And his saying:

*The mothers of people are but vessels,*
*Entrusted [with the seed], while lineages belong to the fathers.*
